title: Equifax Data Breach
summary: One of the largest data breaches in history, exposing the personal information of 147 million Americans including Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and driver's licence numbers. The breach stemmed from an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability.

event_title: Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638 vulnerability publicly disclosed
event_desc: A critical RCE vulnerability in Apache Struts 2 is published. Equifax is notified to patch.
Threat actors exploit the unpatched Struts vulnerability on Equifax's dispute portal to gain a foothold.
Attackers move laterally through 48 unrelated systems, finding unencrypted credentials that allow access to 51 databases. ~147M records exfiltrated over 78 days.
Equifax's security team notices suspicious traffic and begins investigation.
Equifax publicly announces the breach, triggering congressional hearings and a $575M FTC settlement.

title: SolarWinds SUNBURST Supply Chain Attack
summary: A sophisticated nation-state attack (attributed to Russia's SVR) compromised SolarWinds' build pipeline, injecting malicious code into the SUNBURST backdoor distributed via legitimate Orion software updates. Affected 18,000+ organisations including US Treasury, DHS, and Fortune 500 companies.

event_desc: Attackers gain access to SolarWinds' internal development environment, believed to be via a compromised employee password.
SolarWinds releases Orion update 2019.4 – 2020.2.1 containing the SUNBURST backdoor to approximately 18,000 customers.
SUNBURST lies dormant for up to 2 weeks post-install, then beacons to attacker C2 infrastructure disguised as Orion traffic.
FireEye (itself a victim) identifies SUNBURST and notifies CISA and SolarWinds, triggering a coordinated emergency disclosure.
CISA issues Emergency Directive 21-01 ordering all federal agencies to immediately disconnect SolarWinds Orion.

title: Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)
summary: A zero-day RCE vulnerability in the ubiquitous Apache Log4j 2 Java logging library. Triggered by logging a specially crafted string, it enables unauthenticated remote code execution. Rated CVSS 10.0 — the maximum score. Affected hundreds of millions of devices globally.

event_desc: Alibaba Cloud security researcher Chen Zhaojun privately discloses the vulnerability to the Apache Security team.
A proof-of-concept exploit is publicly posted on GitHub before a patch is available, triggering immediate mass exploitation attempts.
Apache releases Log4j 2.15.0 and the CVE is formally assigned. CISA adds it to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue.
Nation-state actors, ransomware groups, and cryptominers all begin exploiting Log4Shell. Cloudflare reports over 1M exploit attempts per minute at peak.
CVE-2021-45046 bypasses the 2.15.0 fix. Apache releases 2.16.0 disabling JNDI by default.

title: WannaCry Ransomware Attack
summary: A global ransomware cyberattack using the EternalBlue exploit (leaked from NSA tooling) targeting unpatched Windows SMBv1. Infected 200,000+ systems across 150 countries in hours. The UK's NHS was severely disrupted. Attributed to North Korea's Lazarus Group.

event_desc: Microsoft patches the EternalBlue SMBv1 vulnerability (CVE-2017-0144), but many organisations fail to apply it.
The Shadow Brokers hacker group leaks NSA offensive tools including EternalBlue publicly online.
WannaCry launches, exploiting EternalBlue to self-propagate across networks. The UK NHS is hit within hours; MRI scanners, theatre equipment, and computers are taken offline.
Security researcher Marcus Hutchins registers an unregistered domain found in the malware's code, inadvertently activating a kill switch that halts new infections.

event_title: US and UK attribute attack to North Korea
event_desc: The US, UK, and Australia formally attribute WannaCry to North Korea's Lazarus Group.

title: PraisonAI Has Path Traversal in FileTools
summary:
### Executive Summary:
The path validation has a critical logic bug: it checks for `..` AFTER `normpath()` has already collapsed all `..` sequences. This makes the check completely useless and allows trivial path traversal to any file on the system.
The path validation function also does not resolve the symlink wich could potentially cause path traversal.

### Details:
`_validate_path()` calls `os.path.normpath()` first, which collapses `..` sequences, then checks for `'..'` in normalized. Since

title: PraisonAI recipe registry publish path traversal allows out-of-root file write
summary:
### Summary

PraisonAI's recipe registry publish endpoint writes uploaded recipe bundles to a filesystem path derived from the bundle's internal `manifest.json` before it verifies that the manifest `name` and `version` match the HTTP route. A malicious publisher can place `../` traversal sequences in the bundle manifest and cause the registry server to create files outside the configured registry root even though the request is ultimately rejected with HTTP `400`.

This is an arbitrary file writ

title: PraisonAI recipe registry pull path traversal writes files outside the chosen output directory
summary:
### Summary

PraisonAI's recipe registry pull flow extracts attacker-controlled `.praison` tar archives with `tar.extractall()` and does not validate archive member paths before extraction. A malicious publisher can upload a recipe bundle that contains `../` traversal entries and any user who later pulls that recipe will write files outside the output directory they selected.

This is a path traversal / arbitrary file write vulnerability on the client side of the recipe registry workflow. It aff

title: PraisonAI Vulnerable to Arbitrary File Write / Path Traversal in Action Orchestrator
summary:
The Action Orchestrator feature contains a Path Traversal vulnerability that allows an attacker (or compromised agent) to write to arbitrary files outside of the configured workspace directory. By supplying relative path segments (`../`) in the target path, malicious actions can overwrite sensitive system files or drop executable payloads on the host.

### Details
Location: `src/praisonai/praisonai/cli/features/action_orchestrator.py` (Lines 402, 409, 423)

Vulnerable Code snippet:
```python
tar

title: PraisonAI Has Arbitrary File Write (Zip Slip) in Templates Extraction
summary:
The PraisonAI templates installation feature is vulnerable to a "Zip Slip" Arbitrary File Write attack. When downloading and extracting template archives from external sources (e.g., GitHub), the application uses Python's `zipfile.extractall()` without verifying if the files within the archive resolve outside of the intended extraction directory. 

### Details
Location: `src/praisonai/praisonai/cli/features/templates.py` (Line 852)

Vulnerable Code snippet:
```python
zip_ref.extractall(tmpdir)
`

title: PraisonAI Has Arbitrary File Write (Zip Slip) in Templates Extraction

Vulnerable Code snippet:
```python

`
event_desc: Vulnerable range: <= 4.5.112

title: go-ipld-prime: DAG-CBOR decoder unbounded memory allocation from CBOR headers
summary:
The DAG-CBOR decoder uses collection sizes declared in CBOR headers as Go preallocation hints for maps and lists. The decoder does not cap these size hints or account for their cost in its allocation budget, allowing small payloads to cause excessive memory allocation.

A CBOR map or list header can declare an arbitrarily large number of entries, causing the decoder to preallocate proportionally large backing structures before any entries are actually read. Because the allocation budget is only

title: go-ipld-prime: DAG-CBOR decoder unbounded memory allocation from CBOR headers

title: PocketMine-MP: Player entities can still die and drop items in flaggedForDespawn state
summary:
### Summary
When an entity dies, the entity is flagged for despawn, but remains in the `World`'s entity table, meaning it's still accessible by doing `World->getEntity($entityId)` and other methods. The same is true of a player when quitting the server.

When a network packet arrives from a client to attack an entity, the handler fetches the entity using `World->getEntity($entityId)` without any checks if the entity is already marked for despawning. Depending on the timing, the entity in questio

title: PocketMine-MP: Network amplification vulnerability with `ActorEventPacket`
summary:
### Impact
The server handles `ActorEventPacket` to trigger consuming animations from vanilla clients when they eat food or drink potions.

This can be abused to make the server spam other clients, and to waste server CPU and memory. For every `ActorEventPacket` sent by the client, an animation event will be sent to every other player the attacker is visible to.

This is similar to various other vulnerabilities which were fixed in the network overhaul of PM4 (e.g. `AnimatePacket` and `LevelSound

title: PocketMine-MP: JSON decoding of unlimited size large arrays/objects in ModalFormResponse Handling
summary:
### Impact

The server does not meaningfully limit the size of the JSON payload in `ModalFormResponsePacket`. This can be abused by an attacker to waste memory and CPU on an affected server, e.g. by sending arrays with millions of elements.

The player must have a full session on the server (i.e. spawned in the world) to exploit this, as form responses are not handled unless the player is in game.

### Patches
The issue was fixed in two parts:
- cef1088341e40ee7a6fa079bca47a84f3524d877 limits th

title: PocketMine-MP: LogDoS by large complex unknown property logging in clientData in LoginPacket
summary:
### Impact

Attackers can put large and/or complex structures as a value to an unknown property in the clientData JWT body in the Minecraft `LoginPacket`, causing the server to generate very long log messages.
Additionally, the property name is logged without any length limitations or sanitization, which can also be abused for LogDoS.

This may be used to spam the log/console, waste CPU time serializing the offending structure, and potentially to crash the server entirely.

This happens because
